bibliographic.citation: Nils Edvard Borgstrom, 'The Complaint of God to Sinful Man and the Answer of Man. By William Lichfield,' Anglia 34 (1911): 498-525.

entities.description: Immediate source article, pp. 498-525; the poem is pp. 508-525. Borgstrom describes Caius 174/95, reconstructs its displaced passage, argues for Lichfield, analyzes language and meter, and prints a close but punctuated edition.

entities.description: Borgstrom's sole direct manuscript source. The poem is physically copied on manuscript pp. 469-480, but the scribe accidentally omitted present lines 321-364 and supplied them later on pp. 481-482 with cross-references to the intended insertion point. Borgstrom's continuous edition follows the corrected intellectual order rather than the codex sequence.
entities.label: Cambridge, Gonville and Caius College, MS 174/95

next_steps: Ask for existing complete photography or a whole-object quotation covering binding, endleaves, blanks, every page, and any separately imaged edges.
Request exact confirmation of the pp. 469-482 pagination and the insertion directions on pp. 476, 481, and 482.
Obtain written terms for private research, public Git redistribution, and commercial or noncommercial publication before sharing supplied images.
openQuestions.question: Can Gonville and Caius College supply or authorize a complete color facsimile of MS 174/95, including the physical pp. 476 and 481-482 evidence for Borgstrom's reordered passage?

next_steps: Consult the complete 2011 article and its witness notes.
Audit later DIMEV revisions, NIMEV corrections, manuscript reidentifications, and all three reported de Worde imprints.
Keep newly identified witnesses collateral unless historical evidence connects them to Borgstrom or another modeled edition.
openQuestions.question: How should DIMEV v1.0's thirteen witnesses be reconciled with Appleford and Watson's statement that the Complaint survives in fourteen manuscripts and two de Worde editions?

next_steps: Check whether DIMEV refers to a later reissue, supplementary pagination, or a simple inherited error.
Retain the directly inspected 1866 pagination at the edition layer and preserve DIMEV's citation as a source-data discrepancy.
Compare all listed Furnivall issues before asserting identical pagination.
openQuestions.question: Why does DIMEV v1.0 cite Furnivall 1866 pp. 198-232 for Lambeth MSS 306 and 853 when the inspected complete 1866 volume prints their parallel Complaint texts on pp. 160-203?

next_steps: Transcribe f. 170v and the adjacent recto and compare both DIMEV incipits and explicits.
Consult Hardman's 2000 facsimile introduction and a current contents handlist.
Until resolved, retain f. 170v / canvas 356 as a shared boundary surface in the whole-codex work portion.
openQuestions.question: Where precisely on NLS Adv.MS.19.3.1 f. 170v does the Complaint end and DIMEV record 2940's Song of Love to Jesus begin?

next_steps: Collate the five cached complete witness facsimiles against Borgstrom's Caius text and Furnivall's Lambeth texts.
Acquire or consult the remaining complete witnesses through their recorded routes.
Distinguish substantive variants, scribal restructuring, early-print revision, attribution history, and editor-supplied readings before adding family edges.
openQuestions.question: What are the textual families, compositional history, and best critical text across the manuscript and early-print tradition?

reviewNotes: Only Caius MS 174/95 is upstream of Borgstrom and CME00089. The twelve other DIMEV witnesses and Furnivall's edition remain collateral supporting relationships.

Complete public facsimiles are cached for Trinity R.3.21, NLS Adv.MS.19.3.1, Lambeth MS 306, Houghton MS Eng 530, and Huntington HM 144. Every complete object retains adjacent works and binding matter, while work_portion records the narrower Complaint range.
No authentication barrier, CAPTCHA, login, provider extraction restriction, or unavailable BL image endpoint was bypassed. Request and subscription routes are recorded instead.
The direct Caius facsimile remains the most important missing source because it is needed to check Borgstrom's italics, line displacement, marginal supplies, and exact page cross-references.

lineage.summary: The repository XML copies U-M CME item CME00089, which encodes only the 544-line poem on pp. 508-525 of Nils Edvard Borgstrom's 1911 Anglia article. Borgstrom directly transcribed Gonville and Caius College MS 174/95 and restored manuscript pp. 481-482's accidentally displaced lines to their signaled position after present line 320. DIMEV v1.0 records thirteen witnesses, but none besides Caius is made an unproved source of Borgstrom. Complete public facsimiles for five collateral witnesses are cached and mapped inside their whole codices: Trinity R.3.21, NLS Adv.MS.19.3.1, Lambeth MS 306, Harvard Houghton MS Eng 530, and Huntington HM 144. Furnivall's complete 1866 volume provides a separately modeled parallel edition of the two Lambeth witnesses.

catalogSummary.author: William Lichfield (attributed; strongly supported)
catalogSummary.date: First half of the fifteenth century; first known in print circa 1510
catalogSummary.region: England, with a strong London association; exact composition place unknown
catalogSummary.title: The Complaint of God

catalogingSubject.label: The Complaint of God
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The sixty-eight-stanza Middle English dialogue conventionally called The Complaint of God and attributed to William Lichfield. The abstract work is kept distinct from its thirteen witnesses in DIMEV v1.0, Wynkyn de Worde's early print, Borgstrom's reordered 1911 transcription of Gonville and Caius MS 174/95, the U-M encoding, the repository XML, and modern facsimiles. CME00089 encodes only Borgstrom's poem on printed pp. 508-525, not his title, manuscript description, attribution discussion, or linguistic study on pp. 498-507.

assertion.notes: This is a broad authorship-based composition window, not the late-fifteenth-century date of Caius 174/95 or Huntington HM 144. No exact year is established.

dateStatements.display: First half of the fifteenth century, before William Lichfield's death in 1447/1448 if the attribution is accepted

assertion.notes: DIMEV records the Cambridge copy as a witness and modern scholarship reports two de Worde editions. The circa-1510 copy is imperfect, and a full early-print census remains desirable.

dateStatements.display: First known in print in Wynkyn de Worde's The remors of conscyence, circa 1510

extent.description: One complete represented poem in 68 LG elements and 544 direct L elements. Eighteen PB elements map Borgstrom's printed pp. 508-525; manuscript-page and poem-line MILESTONE elements total 127. The 206 HI elements chiefly preserve Borgstrom's italic expansion typography and are representation features, not a count of manuscript abbreviations independently verified from the codex.

formStatements.description: Sixty-eight eight-line stanzas, normally rhyming abababab, for 544 lines in the represented Caius version.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Borgstrom's modern English and German linguistic discussion is edition-layer matter outside CME00089's cataloged poem.

Borgstrom calls manuscript pp. 469-482 'folios'; DIMEV and the XML treat the numerals as manuscript pages. The manifest preserves the current scholarly page terminology and records Borgstrom's historical wording in evidence.

The Caius witness's physical displacement of lines 321-364 is not a second work. Borgstrom explains the cross-references and restores the lines to the continuous poem; CME follows that editorial order.
The direct Caius source alone is upstream of Borgstrom. All other witnesses and Furnivall's Lambeth texts are collateral supporting sources, even when complete facsimiles are available.

next_steps: Consult the complete Appleford-Watson argument and subsequent attribution scholarship.
Compare the Caius colophon and attribution annotations in Trinity R.3.21 and Huntington HM 144 palaeographically and historically.
Keep witness dates and London linguistic features separate from composition.
openQuestions.question: Can William Lichfield's authorship and the poem's composition date be narrowed beyond the strong but not unanimous attribution and broad pre-1448 window?

next_steps: Obtain the complete 2011 article's witness list and compare it item by item with DIMEV record 4312.
Separate physical manuscript witnesses, early printed editions, fragments, and lost or reidentified copies.
Update the collateral lineage without retroactively making later census witnesses sources of Borgstrom's edition.
openQuestions.question: Why does DIMEV v1.0 list thirteen witnesses, including the circa-1510 print, while Appleford and Watson report fourteen manuscripts and two de Worde editions?

next_steps: Collate the five publicly available complete witness facsimiles against Borgstrom and Furnivall.
Search recent scholarship by DIMEV, IMEV, NIMEV, incipit, and de Worde title.
Model families and supplied readings only after reading-level evidence is established.
openQuestions.question: How do the thirteen DIMEV witnesses and early prints relate textually, and does a current critical edition supersede the Caius-only Borgstrom text?

assertion.notes: The broad English cultural context is distinct from the production place of any one witness.

Lichfield was rector of All Hallows the Great and Appleford and Watson interpret the writings in relation to London merchants and lay religion. This association is not promoted to a proven composition place; Borgstrom's linguistic comparison with London charters concerns the transmitted language.

Caius 174/95 expressly names Magister William Lychefeld, doctor of theology, in its colophon; Trinity R.3.21 and Huntington HM 144 preserve later attribution history. Appleford and Watson note that unanimity has been lacking but defend Lichfield's authorship. Attribution is therefore strong but not silently converted into an unqualified autograph claim.

notes: Borgstrom transcribed Caius 174/95, supplied punctuation and capitalization policy, made limited corrections, and restored the manuscript's displaced lines 321-364 to their intended continuous position.

subjects.label: Dialogue between God and sinful humanity
Sin, repentance, and amendment of life
Divine mercy, justice, and the Last Judgment
Charity, wealth, trade, and social obligation

summaries.value: God repeatedly confronts sinful humanity with creation, bodily gifts, wealth, reason, Christ's Passion, mercy, and the certainty of judgment. The human speaker confesses failures of lust, avarice, false dealing, oaths, anger, and neglect of the poor, then promises restitution, charity, penance, and amendment. The alternating admonitions and replies join pastoral instruction to penitential devotion, with particular attention to the moral obligations of ordinary social and commercial life.

CME00089 represents Borgstrom's reconstructed continuous reading order. In Caius 174/95 the scribe accidentally omitted present lines 321-364, copied them later on manuscript pp. 481-482, and marked the intended insertion point; the abstract work and the witness's physical order therefore must not be conflated.

assertion.notes: DIMEV's established short title; the definite article is supplied for display.

titles.value: The Complaint of God to Sinful Man and the Answer of Man
Owre gracious god, prynce of pite
That leve there synne and them amende
